cases.prompt: harden our npm dependencies
protect this repo from malicious packages
set up dependabot with a cooldown
should we block postinstall scripts?
audit the dependency tree
lock down npm tokens for the org
pin our github actions to commit SHAs
verify provenance of our dependencies
another npm worm in the news - are we exposed?
publish our package with provenance
enable secret scanning on the repo
set up the CI matrix
fix this CVE in our own code
add eslint security rules
configure package exports
rotate this leaked AWS key
upgrade react to the latest version
harden this repo's supply chain
expected_behavior: layers the real controls: committed lockfile + npm ci, Dependabot weekly grouped updates with cooldown (security updates bypass it by design; transitive npm deps not covered), install-script policy (npm v12 blocks lifecycle scripts by default / --ignore-scripts), SHA-pinned third-party actions
explicitly warns against blanket auto-merge of bot PRs (bot-opened PRs have delivered malware on green CI)
distinguishes what this skill owns (npm/dependency hygiene) from repo settings like rulesets/secret scanning and routes those elsewhere
verifies with the skill's audit script or npm audit signatures

cases.prompt: a dependency we use was compromised upstream - respond
expected_behavior: checks exposure precisely (lockfile resolution, affected version ranges, install-script execution window) before mass-upgrading
pins or overrides to a known-good version and documents the incident window
recommends cooldown + provenance verification going forward, not panic-pinning everything forever

cases.prompt: set up dependabot for this pnpm monorepo
expected_behavior: configures grouped weekly updates with cooldown for npm ecosystem + github-actions
notes cooldown does not apply to transitive deps and security PRs bypass it
requires CI + review before merge on bot PRs (no blanket auto-merge)
